topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the glossy presentation slides flickering on the conference room screen. The numbers—the projected synergy, the acquisition targets—felt like a deliberate assault on his composure. He gripped the edge of the mahogany table so tightly his knuckles bleached white. Every word spoken by Mr. Henderson, smooth and reassuring, grated against his nerves like sandpaper. "This is a strategic realignment, Elias," Henderson had purred, leaning back with an air of supreme confidence. Elias just nodded, a tight, jerky movement, his jaw clenched so hard a dull ache radiated through his temples. He felt a cold, hollow sensation spreading through his chest, a deep, unsettling emptiness where his usual professional engagement used to reside. He began methodically packing his laptop, the click of the case closing sounding unnaturally loud in the sudden silence. He didn't look at anyone; he just needed to get out of that suffocating room.

[story 2]

Sarah traced the grain of the desk with a fingertip, the wood cool beneath her touch. The air in the office felt thick, stagnant, suffocating. She watched Mark pace near the window, his movements sharp and agitated, his shoulders constantly hunched. When the CEO finally dropped the bombshell—that the company was being sold—Sarah felt a sudden, sharp tightening in her throat. She forced a small, brittle smile, the muscles in her face feeling alien and stiff. "Right," she managed, her voice sounding thin and reedy. Internally, a furious churning began in her gut, a hot, unwelcome pressure that made her want to bolt. She stood up abruptly, the chair scraping back against the floor. "I'll need to review the transition documents immediately," she stated, her tone clipped, utterly devoid of warmth. She didn't wait for a reply, just turned and walked toward the elevator, each step feeling heavy with suppressed energy.

[story 3]

From his cubicle, David watched the flurry of activity in the hallway. People were whispering, their faces a mixture of shock and grim calculation. David felt a slow, creeping sense of dread settle over him, heavy and inescapable. He picked up a stray paperclip, turning it over and over in his hand until it felt slick. The news had hit him like a physical blow—the entire structure he had built his career upon was dissolving. He stood up, his movements slow and deliberate, like someone wading through deep water. When his manager approached, David met his gaze, his eyes flat and unreadable. "So," David said, his voice low, almost a monotone, "what exactly does this mean for the rest of us?" There was no inflection of surprise, only a profound, chilling resignation that settled deep within his bones. He simply waited, radiating a stillness that screamed volumes.
